model_input:
Question: An otherwise healthy man in his 40s was referred to our dermatology clinic for a slowly growing tumor on the scalp. Skin examination revealed an 8-mm red, firm, mobile nodule on the left temporal scalp. On dermoscopic evaluation, irregular red lacunae with irregular vessels and absence of septa were observed (Figure 1A and B). The lesion was excised for histopathologic analysis. What Is Your Diagnosis?
Options: A:Tufted angioma B:Masson tumor C:Glomeruloid hemangioma D:Papillary hemangioma
 Which option is the correct answer to the question? ALWAYS provide the option index of the final answer AT THE END with TRIPLE BACKTICKS among A, B, C, D, EXACTLY like this: The answer is ```X```. DO NOT add any other text or variations.
caption: Dermoscopic findings of an 8-mm red nodule on the scalp show irregular red lacunae without septa and irregular linear vessels (A), and hematoxylin-eosin staining shows a vascular proliferation in dermis and subcutis composed of ectatic thin-walled vessels containing multiple branching structures (B).
Periodic acid Schiff staining shows multiple hyaline positive globules in the cytoplasm of endothelial cells.
explanation: Hematoxylin-eosin staining of the lesion demonstrated a well-circumscribed dermal vascular tumor extending to the superficial subcutaneous tissue and composed of ectatic thin-walled vessels with multiple intravascular branching papillary structures (Figure 1B). These structures were covered by swollen endothelial cells with numerous intracytoplasmic eosinophilic hyaline globules, positive on periodic acid Schiff (PAS) (Figure 2). The papillae core contained collagenous stroma, pericytes, and normal-appearing capillary vessels. Tumor vessels were positive for CD-31, and negative for D2-40 (podoplanin) and HHV-8;  -smooth muscle actin highlighted the pericytes in the papillae. The basal membrane and stroma inside the papillae were positive for collagen IV.Periodic acid Schiff staining shows multiple hyaline positive globules in the cytoplasm of endothelial cells.Papillary hemangioma (PH) is a benign tumor that was first described in 2007 by Suurmeijer et al.1 To our knowledge, only 16 cases have been described in the English-language literature.1-4 Most of the reported cases have occurred in male adults on the head and neck.2 Histopathologic mimickers of PH include tufted angioma (TA), Masson tumor (MT), intravascular papillary endothelial hyperplasia, and glomeruloid hemangioma (GH), among others. Most of these neoplasms exhibit intravascular capillary proliferations similar to PH.TA differs from PH in that it presents as a slow-growing plaque, most frequently in children and young adults. In some cases, this lesion has been associated with hyperhidrosis or hypertricosis. On dermoscopic examination, skin colored to light pink structureless areas have been described. TA may develop Kasabach-Merritt phenomena, which does not occur in PH. Histopathologically, TA presents multiple lobules of packed capillaries scattered throughout the dermis and the subcutis separated by normal stroma (cannonball pattern). Unlike PH, papillary structures or hyaline globules are not seen.MT is a reactive proliferation of endothelial cells secondary to an unusual thrombus within a vein, a proliferation inside a preexisting vascular lesion, or rarely, associated with a hematoma. MT affects young adults and presents as a bluish or red nodule or papule on the extremities.5 Histologically, the papillary structures of MT are characterized by an acellular fibrous core coated by endothelial cells. These papillae lack capillaries, and it is sometimes possible to observe thrombosis in different stages of organization within the dilated vessel.GH is the most important PH histopathological differential diagnosis because both conditions show a proliferation of capillary vessels within a dilated vascular structure and hyaline globules; however, its clinical presentation and associated diseases differ. Clinically, GH presents as multiple, small, red papules on the trunk and limbs, with a rapid onset of days or few weeks.6,7 In contrast, PH presents in adults as a solitary tumor on the head and neck. Additionally, GH is considered to be a hallmark finding of POEMS (polyneuropathy, organomegaly, endocrinopathy, monoclonal plasma cell disorder, skin changes)6 and TAFRO (thrombocytopenia, anasarca, fever, reticulin fibrosis, and organomegaly) syndromes. PH has no associated underlying diseases. Cases reported as solitary GH without associated disease are likely PH.2,8On dermoscopic examination, GH displays red lacunae and/or red-white backgrounds, linear irregular vessels, and red rounded structures.6,7 In the present case, PH dermoscopic findings showed red lacunae without septa, white structureless areas, and irregular vessels. These findings have not been previously described. Histopathologically, there are important architectural differences among these. GH is a superficial dermal proliferation composed of a big sinusoidal central vessel with multiple capillaries inside forming loops, which resemble a kidney glomerulus. GH lacks a prominent stroma and substantial pericytes.8 PH is a deep dermal to subcutaneous tumor composed of interconnected intravascular papillae with abundant stroma, pericytes, and scant capillaries. In both GH and PH, swollen endothelial cells with hyaline globules may be observed. These globules correspond to thanatosomes, secondary lysosomes that would represent cellular damage.9In some cases, differentiating GH from PH can be challenging. Suurmeijer et al8 reported that the best immunohistochemical marker to differentiate GH from PH in complex cases is collagen IV, which is abundant in the stroma and shows a thick basement membrane in PH and a thin basement membrane in GH. Recently, somatic variations in GNA11 and GNAQ were described in pediatric cases of PH. GNA11 and GNAQ variants could be an aid in PH differential diagnosis.10 In conclusion, we report a case of PH with its clinical and histologic characteristics and describe the dermoscopic features of PH. It is important for clinicians to be familiar with this entity because confusion with GH may lead to unnecessary laboratory testing.
Question: An otherwise healthy man in his 40s was referred to our dermatology clinic for a slowly growing tumor on the scalp. Skin examination revealed an 8-mm red, firm, mobile nodule on the left temporal scalp. On dermoscopic evaluation, irregular red lacunae with irregular vessels and absence of septa were observed (Figure 1A and B). The lesion was excised for histopathologic analysis. What Is Your Diagnosis?
raw_response:
Okay, so I'm trying to figure out the correct diagnosis for this dermatology case. Let me start by going through the information given.

The patient is a 40s man who's otherwise healthy and was referred for a slowly growing tumor on his scalp. The initial skin exam shows an 8-mm red, firm, mobile nodule on the left temporal scalp. Then, under dermoscopy, they see irregular red lacunae with irregular vessels and no septa. The options are Tufted angioma, Masson tumor, Glomeruloid hemangioma, and Papillary hemangioma.

First, I need to recall what each of these lesions looks like under dermoscopy. 

Tufted angioma usually has a patchy, red, and firm appearance, with well-defined borders. They often have a tufted appearance, hence the name. The vessels in these are usually small and may be tortuous but still well-defined.

Masson tumor is a more aggressive, indurated, and multinodular lesion. It often has septae, which are the dividing walls between the lobules. The color can be more yellowish or grayish, and the vessels are usually more prominent and less organized.

Glomeruloid hemangioma, also known as glomerular hemangioma, typically presents as a small, red, indurated nodule. The dermoscopy shows a solid mass with well-defined borders. The vessels here are usually irregular and may be more like a network without clear septa. They can be associated with minimal kidney involvement, hence the name.

Papillary hemangioma is characterized by a thin, papery appearance. The dermoscopy shows a thin, flat, red nodule with well-defined borders. The vessels are usually small and may be tortuous but still within a thin layer.

In the case presented, the lesion is 8 mm, red, firm, mobile. Under dermoscopy, it shows irregular red lacunae with irregular vessels and no septa. This description matches the features of a glomeruloid hemangioma. The absence of septa is a key point because Masson tumors typically have septae. The irregular vessels and lacunae also support this diagnosis.

I should also consider the other options. Tufted angioma would have well-defined borders and possibly some tufting, which isn't mentioned here. Papillary hemangioma would be thinner and flatter, which doesn't fit the description. Masson tumor has septae, which are absent here.

So, putting it all together, the correct diagnosis is Glomeruloid hemangioma, which is option C.
</think>

The correct diagnosis is Glomeruloid hemangioma, which corresponds to option C.

The answer is ```C```
